# Alassane Ouattara Stadium

Alassane Ouattara Stadium

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4f/Stade_Olympique_Alassane_Ouattara_d%27Ebimp%C3%A9_Abidjan.jpg/250px-Stade_Olympique_Alassane_Ouattara_d%27Ebimp%C3%A9_Abidjan.jpg)

![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,13,a,a,250x200.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Alassane_Ouattara_Stadium&revid=1337000027&groups=_a530eedcb62ed2616ba375782dcf98b3048968d0&parser=legacy)

**Alassane Ouattara Stadium**, commonly known as the **Olympic Stadium of Ebimpé** and formerly as the **National Stadium of the Ivory Coast**, is a multi-purpose stadium in Ebimpé and [Anyama](/wiki/Anyama "Anyama"), in northern [Abidjan](/wiki/Abidjan "Abidjan"). It opened in 2020.[[2]](#cite_note-Stadium_presentation-2) The stadium hosts major football matches including home matches of the [Ivory Coast national football team](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_national_football_team "Ivory Coast national football team"). Alassane Ouattara Stadium is owned by the [Government of Ivory Coast](/wiki/Government_of_Ivory_Coast "Government of Ivory Coast"). With 60,000 seats, it is the [largest stadium in Ivory Coast](/wiki/List_of_football_stadiums_in_Ivory_Coast "List of football stadiums in Ivory Coast") and [one of the most modern stadiums in Africa](/wiki/List_of_African_stadiums_by_capacity "List of African stadiums by capacity").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Designed by the Beijing Institute Architectural Design The [new national stadium of Ivory Coast](/wiki/List_of_national_stadiums "List of national stadiums") was strategically situated away from Abidjan, positioned between the communes of Ebimpé and [Anyama](/wiki/Anyama "Anyama"). In a direct distance, it stands approximately 20 km north of central [Abidjan](/wiki/Abidjan "Abidjan"). The decision to relocate the country's sporting hub beyond the city limits was a deliberate one. Ultimately, it is envisioned to be an integral part of a sprawling 287-hectare sports city, set to become one of the largest sports and leisure districts in Africa to host multiple sports like [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") and [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)"). As for the stadium itself, the initial construction phase covered only its immediate surroundings, encompassing four plazas and providing 1,400 [parking spaces](/wiki/Parking_space "Parking space"). The stadium is situated on a rectangular plot spanning 20 hectares. Due to variations in land elevation, the project necessitated extensive excavation, involving the removal of approximately 500,000 tons of [soil](/wiki/Soil "Soil"). This early stage presented considerable challenges, exacerbated by heavy rainfall that led to instances of [mud flooding](/wiki/Muddy_flood "Muddy flood").

The stadium was primarily designed and its construction was initiated with the intention of hosting the [2021 Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/2021_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2021 Africa Cup of Nations"), under the leadership of Prime Minister [Daniel Kablan Duncan](/wiki/Daniel_Kablan_Duncan "Daniel Kablan Duncan"), but On 30 November 2018, CAF stripped Cameroon of hosting the [2019 Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/2019_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2019 Africa Cup of Nations") because of delays in the construction of stadiums and other necessary infrastructure;[[4]](#cite_note-4) it was relocated to Egypt.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Then-CAF President [Ahmad Ahmad](/wiki/Ahmad_Ahmad "Ahmad Ahmad") said that Cameroon had agreed to host the 2021 tournament instead.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Consequently, Ivory Coast, the original hosts of 2021, hosted the [2023 Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/2023_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2023 Africa Cup of Nations"). On 30 January 2019, the CAF President confirmed the timetable shift, after a meeting with Ivory Coast President, [Alassane Ouattara](/wiki/Alassane_Ouattara "Alassane Ouattara"), in [Abidjan](/wiki/Abidjan "Abidjan"), Ivory Coast.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The 2023 Africa Cup of Nations is the first major competition hosted in the country since the [1984 edition](/wiki/1984_African_Cup_of_Nations "1984 African Cup of Nations").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## History

### Construction

The ground was broken for the stadium on 22 December 2016 by Prime Minister [Daniel Kablan Duncan](/wiki/Daniel_Kablan_Duncan "Daniel Kablan Duncan"), with the presence of a China embassy representative.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) In keeping with the common practice in [Chinese–African partnerships](/wiki/Sino-African_relations "Sino-African relations") in [stadium diplomacy](/wiki/Stadium_diplomacy "Stadium diplomacy"), most of the work on the stadium was carried out by Chinese companies, with an array of Chinese brands involved in the project. The design documentation for the stadium was prepared by the Beijing Institute of Architectural Design (BIAD), while the construction itself was executed by the [Beijing Construction Engineering Group](/wiki/Beijing_Construction_Engineering_Group "Beijing Construction Engineering Group"). From the early stages of conceptual design, the stadium earned its nickname, the "Arc de Triomphe." This moniker was bestowed due to the presence of 96 columns encircling the entire structure, creating a dynamic arcade adorned with stained glass in the national colors. These columns serve both as a striking façade and as support for the roof. At its peak, the stadium reaches a height of 51.4 meters, with approximate dimensions of 290 meters in length and 270 meters in width.The stadium boasts an impressive blueprint covering over 61,250 square meters and offers five floors of facilities, particularly concentrated within the main stand. The seating bowl is divided into three tiers, each providing 24 rows, 13 rows, and up to 28 rows from bottom to top, respectively. Only the third tier is not a continuous ring, featuring openings in all corners. Despite the auditorium being topped by 32 spacious roof girders, the roof fully covers only the third tier and part of the second tier.

Official construction activities commenced on December 22, 2016, with an initial delivery deadline set for October 2019, totaling 34 months for completion. At its peak, the construction site saw around 1,500 individuals working, with approximately 400 of them being Chinese personnel. However, the project experienced significant delays, leading to the stadium's completion only in late spring of 2020. Several factors contributed to the delay, including adverse weather conditions, a considerable number of malaria cases among the Chinese workforce, and the impact of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") late in the project timeline. Consequently, the opening date was postponed to October 2020. Due to the international collaboration involved in the stadium's construction, the Ivorian government was responsible for covering only a portion of the project's cost, with the remaining funds provided by China. The reported total cost of the stadium was XOF 143 billion (approximately $257 million at the time of opening), with China contributing 63 billion.With a seating capacity of just over 60,000, the stadium is nearly twice the size of Ivory Coast's previous national stadium, [Felix Houphouet Boigny Stadium](/wiki/Felix_Houphouet_Boigny_Stadium "Felix Houphouet Boigny Stadium"). Following its inauguration, it became one of the largest stadiums in West Africa. In November 2019, the stadium was near its completion. It was expected to be delivered to the government of Ivory Coast in February 2020.[[11]](#cite_note-11) However, there was a delay due to the weather and health issues, including the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Ivory_Coast "COVID-19 pandemic in Ivory Coast").[[2]](#cite_note-Stadium_presentation-2)

### Inauguration

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9c/Stade_d%27Ebimp%C3%A9.jpg/250px-Stade_d%27Ebimp%C3%A9.jpg)

The stadium was inaugurated on 3 October 2020 and was named after President [Alassane Ouattara](/wiki/Alassane_Ouattara "Alassane Ouattara"). Many officials and the Chinese ambassador in Ivory Coast were also present at a party in the stadium which contained a show of dance and music. A friendly match was also played after the show between the two most popular clubs in Ivory Coast and Africa, [ASEC Mimosas](/wiki/ASEC_Mimosas "ASEC Mimosas") and [Africa Sports d'Abidjan](/wiki/Africa_Sports_d%27Abidjan "Africa Sports d'Abidjan"). ASEC Mimosas won the match 2–0.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## 2023 Africa Cup of Nations

The stadium is one of the venues for the [2023 Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/2023_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2023 Africa Cup of Nations").

The following matches are being played at the stadium:
